text: An AM wave has 1800 W of total power content. For 100% modulation, the carrier power content should be
explanation:
For AM wave, total power:

\[P_t = P_c\left(1+\frac{m^2}{2}\right)\]

For 100% modulation, \(m=1\):

\[1800 = P_c\left(1+\frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{3}{2}P_c\]

\[P_c = \frac{2}{3}\times1800 = 1200\text{ W}\]
name: AM Wave Carrier Power for 100% Modulation

text: A speech signal of 3 kHz is used to modulate a carrier of 1 MHz using amplitude modulation. The sideband frequencies are
explanation:
Upper sideband \(= f_c + f_m = 1\text{ MHz} + 0.003\text{ MHz} = 1.003\text{ MHz}\)

Lower sideband \(= f_c - f_m = 1\text{ MHz} - 0.003\text{ MHz} = 0.997\text{ MHz}\)
name: AM Sideband Frequencies

text: A signal of frequency 10 kHz and peak voltage 10 V modulates a carrier of frequency 1 MHz and peak voltage 20 V. The sideband frequencies (in kHz) are
explanation:
Sideband frequencies \(= f_c \pm f_s = 1000\text{ kHz} \pm 10\text{ kHz}\)

\(= 1010\text{ kHz}\) and \(990\text{ kHz}\)
name: AM Sideband Frequencies from Signal Parameters

text: A TV tower has height 5 m. Population density is \(1000/\pi\) per km². Number of people (in thousands) that can receive the transmission is nearly (\(R_e = 6400\) km)
explanation:
Range \(d = \sqrt{2hR_e}\)

Area \(A = \pi d^2 = 2\pi h R_e\)

Population \(= n \times A = \dfrac{1000}{\pi} \times 2\pi h R_e\)

\(= 2000 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 6400 = 64000\)

So **64 thousand** people.
name: TV Tower Coverage — Population

text: A transmitting antenna of height 20 m and receiving antenna of height \(h\) are 40 km apart in LOS mode. The value of \(h\) is (\(R_e = 6400\) km)
explanation:
\[d_m = \sqrt{2Rh_T} + \sqrt{2Rh_R}\]

\[40 = \sqrt{2\times6400\times0.020} + \sqrt{2\times6400\times h_R}\]

\[40 = 16 + \sqrt{12800 h_R}\]

\[24 = \sqrt{12800 h_R} \implies h_R = \frac{576}{12800} \approx 0.045\text{ km} = 45\text{ m}\]
name: Line-of-Sight Communication — Antenna Height

text: The frequency suitable for beyond-the-horizon communication using sky waves is
explanation: Sky waves are radio waves reflected by the ionosphere. The frequency range used for sky wave (beyond-the-horizon) communication is the HF band: \(3\text{ MHz}\) to \(30\text{ MHz}\), i.e., around \(10^7\text{ Hz}\).
name: Sky Wave Communication Frequency

text: A TV tower has height 160 m. Its coverage range is nearly (\(R_e = 6400\) km)
name: TV Tower Coverage Range

text: Co-axial cable, widely used for signal transmission, offers a bandwidth of approximately
explanation:
Coaxial cable is used for transmission of radio and TV signals in the range 3 MHz to 3 GHz.

Bandwidth \(\approx 3000\text{ MHz} - 3\text{ MHz} \approx 3000\text{ MHz}\).

The nearest given option is **750 MHz**.
name: Coaxial Cable Bandwidth

text: If \(E_c\) and \(E_m\) are peak values of carrier and modulating signals respectively, then for 100% modulation
explanation:
Modulation index \(\mu = \dfrac{E_m}{E_c}\)

For 100% modulation, \(\mu = 1\implies E_m = E_c\)
name: 100% Modulation Condition

text: A message signal of peak voltage 12 V modulates a carrier at modulation index 0.6. To increase modulation index to 0.75, the carrier peak voltage should be
explanation:
\(M = V_{signal}/V_{carrier}\)

Initial: \(0.6 = 12/V_1 \implies V_1 = 20\text{ V}\)

Final: \(0.75 = 12/V_2 \implies V_2 = 16\text{ V}\)

Change: \(\Delta V = 20-16 = 4\text{ V}\)

\(\% \text{ change} = 4/20 \times 100 = 20\%\text{ decrease}\)
name: Modulation Index Change

text: The maximum distance between transmitting and receiving TV towers is 65 km. If the ratio of heights of transmitting to receiving tower is 36:49, the heights are (\(R_e = 6400\) km)
explanation:
Given \(h_T/h_R = 36/49\) and \(d = 65 = \sqrt{2Rh_T}+\sqrt{2Rh_R}\).

Let \(h_T = 36k\), \(h_R = 49k\). Solving: \(h_T = 70.3\text{ m}\), \(h_R = 95.7\text{ m}\).
name: TV Tower Heights from LOS Distance

text: A TV transmitter has a range of 50 km. The height of the transmitter is (\(R_e = 6.4\times10^6\) m)
name: TV Transmitter Height from Range

text: If the height of a transmitting tower is increased by 30%, then the area covered by it increases by
explanation:
Coverage area \(A = \pi d^2 = 2\pi R h\)

So \(A \propto h\).

If \(h\) increases by 30%, area also increases by **30%**.
name: Coverage Area vs Tower Height

text: A modulated signal is \(C_m(t)=A_c\sin\omega_c t+\mu A_c\sin\omega_m t\sin\omega_c t\). To keep the signal without distortion, the modulation index \(\mu\) should be
explanation:
Modulation index \(\mu = A_m/A_c\).

For distortion-free transmission, the amplitude of the modulated signal must remain non-negative:

\(A_m \leq A_c \implies \mu \leq 1\)

Over-modulation (\(\mu>1\)) causes distortion.
name: Modulation Index for Distortion-Free Signal
